topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red 'D-' slapped against the stark white of the paper, a physical blow that stole the air from my lungs. I stared at the mark, the ink seeming to mock my efforts. My hands, which had been steady during the exam, started to tremble, and I quickly folded the paper, shoving it deep into my backpack as if it were contaminated. Walking out of the classroom felt like wading through thick mud; every glance from a passing peer felt like an accusation. I kept my head down, focusing intently on the scuffed linoleum floor, desperate to become invisible. Later, alone in my room, I couldn't bring myself to open the book again. The silence was heavy, punctuated only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs.

[story 2]

"Seriously? A C-minus?" The question echoed in my head, sharp and unforgiving. I picked up the graded essay, the paper feeling flimsy and inadequate in my grasp. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I traced a line on the margin with my index finger, the motion jerky and uncontrolled. I avoided looking at my reflection in the darkened window; the image staring back seemed pale and defeated. When my roommate knocked on the door, I forced a bright, almost brittle smile, the kind that doesn't reach my eyes. "Just a rough day," I mumbled, the words sounding hollow even to my own ears. I spent the rest of the evening meticulously cleaning my desk, a pointless ritual meant to scrub away the evidence of my failure.

[story 3]

The notification popped up on my phone, a digital verdict delivered with cold finality. I reread the score three times, the numbers swimming before my eyes. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck and spread across my cheeks. I stood by my locker, leaning against the cold metal, trying to physically shrink my frame. Every sound in the hallway—the chatter, the slamming doors—seemed amplified, judging the inadequacy of my performance. I clutched my textbooks so tightly my knuckles turned white. When I finally managed to slip into the library, I didn't go to study. Instead, I sat at a far corner table, staring blankly at a textbook, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. The weight of expectation felt crushing, pressing down on my chest until breathing became a conscious, shallow effort.
